passage:
A market researcher surveyed consumers about their willingness to pay a premium (defined as more than 20% above standard price) for products labeled with various sustainability certifications.

| Certification Label | % Willing to Pay Premium |
|---|---|
| Organic | 54 |
| Fair Trade | 48 |
| Carbon Neutral | 41 |
| Rainforest Alliance | 38 |
| No Label | 12 |

The researcher concluded that sustainability certifications, regardless of type, significantly increase consumer willingness to pay a premium compared to unlabeled products.
question: Which finding most directly supports the researcher's conclusion?

choices.A: The Organic label has the highest premium willingness at 54%.
choices.B: Every sustainability certification—Organic (54%), Fair Trade (48%), Carbon Neutral (41%), and Rainforest Alliance (38%)—has a substantially higher premium willingness than the unlabeled baseline of 12%.
choices.C: Fair Trade and Carbon Neutral labels have similar premium willingness rates.
choices.D: The Rainforest Alliance label has the lowest premium willingness among the certified options at 38%.

explanation: The researcher concludes that sustainability certifications, 'regardless of type,' increase willingness to pay a premium 'significantly' compared to unlabeled products. This claim is about all certification types and their comparison to the no-label baseline. Choice B directly addresses both parts: it confirms that every certification—all four types—shows substantially higher premium willingness than the 12% baseline, with gaps ranging from 26 to 42 percentage points. This supports the 'regardless of type' and 'significantly' aspects of the conclusion. Choice A addresses only one label. Choice C notes a similarity between two labels without comparing them to the baseline. Choice D identifies the lowest-performing certification without comparing it to the unlabeled baseline or addressing the full scope of the claim.
